sentences.text: My name is Paul Kuttner.
K-U-T-T-N-E-R.
No, I do not.
I was born in Berlin, Germany.
B-E-R-L-I-N.
September 20, 1922.
Also Paul Kuttner.
Margarete Kuttner, and her maiden name was Margarete Fraenkel.
He was a medical doctor.
My mother was a nurse in World War I.
But then she became a housewife and mother.
She taught piano lessons, private piano lessons.
Yes, I had one sister, Anne Marie, who was born in 1912.
And I had another brother who was born 1918 or 1919, but he died a few weeks after his birth.
Well, first of all, what I remember is that I lived in the [GERMAN] 14.
And my father and my mother were still together there.
And around 1929 or so, he left my mother for another woman by the name of Ida, or Ida, Kasper-- a very nice woman, I must say.
But I was very, very unhappy about that separation.
And my mother suffered terribly-- that I remember.
But after a few years, she got used to that too, I suppose.
And we moved from the [GERMAN] to the Kaiser Allee.
Which is just a few blocks away.
And that's where I live for the rest of my stay in Germany until I left in 1939.
And I lived with my mother and my sister, Anne Marie, in the Kaiser Allee.
Very much so.
Every weekend, either on Saturdays or Sundays, and sometimes on Saturdays and Sundays, I had to visit him and his wife, Ida.
But I must say they were wonderful to me in every respect.
They never said anything against my mother, who was the greatest love of my life, as a matter of fact.
And I must say they were just wonderful to me in every respect.
Not just because they fed me, but they really seemed to love me.
No, I did not.
My parents separated about 1928 or 1929.
I was too young to remember the exact date, but it was before 1930.
Well, we lived in a sort of a luxurious apartment while my father still had his medical practice there in the [GERMAN].
After he-- my parents separated, I [SIGHS] noticed that we had to save more money for the simple reason that we were dependent on his monthly payments.
Probably the divorce agreement made this arrangement.
And I have no idea about the financial terms were, but it was enough for my mother, my sister, and me to keep above water.
And I never missed a meal.
But later on, we had to leave the large apartment in the [GERMAN] and move to the Kaiser [INAUDIBLE].
It was a smaller apartment, but still a very, very nice apartment.
And in order to survive a little bit better, my mother let a few-- a couple of rooms.
I started in about-- when I was 6 years old, 1928, I went to the [GERMAN], which is a sort of a primary school for four years.
And my teacher was Mr.
Hoffman, who was a very, very nice guy and had a great influence on my life because of his goodness and patience with all of his students.
And after that, I went to the Bismarck Gymnasium and stayed there until the Kristallnacht in November 1938.
Yeah, it was a German gymnasium.
Till 1938.
Yeah, there was no questions ever asked about my religion.
And I just stayed there until November.
Some of-- some of the students may have been aware that I was a Jew.
And of course, the fact is that I did not join either the Hitler Youth or the Jungvolk.
That's the organization before you join the Hitler Youth.
Most of them actually had joined, but they were still quite a few Christian or Aryan boys who did not join.
And I don't know if they ever did join, but, so I was not sticking out like a sore thumb in a big crowd.
And I never felt any backlash as far as my religion was concerned.
No, to my knowledge, there were none, at least in my class.
I don't know about the rest of the school.
Well, the-- before Hitler came to power in 1933, I felt absolutely no anti-semitism.
It was simply, as far as I was concerned as a child, was not aware of it.
And just never saw any overt signs of anti-semitism.
Afterwards, I must say that, in my school, I did not experience any-- any anti-semitism either, except for one incident of a man who lived above us who was a member of the SS.
And he had fallen in love with my sister, who was 10 years older than I am, Anne Marie.
And he tried to court her, even though he was married himself.
And when one day he visited us, I had to tell him, around 1935 or 1936-- I can't remember the exact year-- that we were Jewish.
And he got such a shock that he slapped me and called me a [GERMAN] and simply said that he didn't want to have anything to do with us anymore and left.
And that was the last we saw of him.
In-- in no way.
Non-- we were not affiliated with anything.
As far as religion was concerned, it was not mentioned.
We had no-- nothing to say in that matter, absolutely nothing.
I have nothing to say in that matter, either.
No, No, not in any way either disassociate or any other way.
We just lived the way you and I talk, and religion did not enter our lives.
Well, that's very easy because the Nuremberg Laws of 1935, first of all, deprived my father of all his Christian clientele, his patients could no longer visit him, only Jewish patients.
So from a professional point-of-view, it was very, very rough on him and he suffered tremendously.
And of course he was no longer permitted even to visit sick patients who were not Jewish.
And apparently, it had such-- it had such an effect on him that he had a stroke in about 1937, shortly after the Olympic games in Berlin of 1936.
As far as my mother was concerned, she could still have her-- oddly enough-- still have her Christian or Aryan renters, the ones who lodged with her.
And these people knew about us.
And except for one incident, we never had any problem with them.
In fact, there was one man, his name was Winkelman, who happened to be a hunchbacked little artist.
And he was responsible mostly for sketching and drawing swastikas, swastika emblems, for the various Nazi parties in Germany.
And he was violently anti-Nazi, but that's how he made his-- his living.
On the-- next to him lived a Fraulein Mueller, a Miss Mueller, who was very anti-- who was very pro-Nazi.
And one day when I was sick, I was visited by three members of the Gestapo.
Of course, I was sick in bed with the flu.
I remember that.
It was-- that was about 1936 or 1937.
And apparently Miss Mueller had revealed to them that I had made anti-Hitler statements to my mother or to somebody at home.
And they came to interrogate me.
And of course, I had to deny it.
And the only way I could deny it is by actually pointing out that I had-- and I'll explain to you why in a little while-- that I had a picture of Hitler on my desk.
This picture was actually given to me by a friend of mine, Jurgen Franke, who used to live under us, who was a member of the Jungvolk, or Hitler Youth.
And he was a very, very nice guy, but a great Nazi.
And he thought he could make me happy by giving me a picture of Hitler.
And so I showed them this-- pointed my finger to that picture and said, why would I make anti-Nazi statement if I had a picture of Hitler right there on my desk.
And they were quite surprised.
And then another thing happened.
We had a Latin teacher in my school by the name of Dr.
Schwertweger And he was an extremely good-looking man.
And the Nazis used his profile as propaganda for how to show how the Aryan race, what the Aryan race looked like.
And since he was my Latin teacher, he gave each of us his photo, and in his-- as a uniform.
So I had this on my desk, too.
And I said, how could I be anti-Hitler when I have a picture of Hitler on my desk and a picture of this as [INAUDIBLE].
And the three Gestapo officers apologized to me and said, of course, you are absolutely right.
Why would you make an anti-Nazi statement.
And they asked, actually, Fraulein Mueller to leave my mother's apartment and move somewhere else.
And that saved me.
Did they know at that time that you were Jewish?
They asked you whether you were Jewish?
They may have asked me.
I cannot remember if they did that.
They may have asked me, but I cannot remember.
Well, he really-- he lost all, as I told you, he lost most of his practice.
And he had to support his wife, his new wife, and also my mother and my sister and me.
And he felt he couldn't manage financially and he got terribly upset, and himself did not visit a doctor.
And I noticed that his face became redder and redder from the high blood pressure.
And one day when I came back from my vacation in Hamburg where I always visited my grandmother, my mother told me that my father had had a stroke.
At first, I was not permitted to see him because it was a very, very bad stroke, apparently.
And, but finally after about two months or so, I was permitted to visit him.
And he could hardly talk.
He was completely lame on one side and stayed that way until I left Germany in February 1939.
But later he did improve.
And I could still talk to him, probably the middle of 1938.
Well--
I could visit him.
At first, I could only visit him in the [GERMAN] Sanatorium, where a Professor Gorbandt was actually the chief, and he was an Aryan.
Now the thing is, my father, being Jewish, really should have stayed in a Jewish hospital.
But since Gorbandt, who really had a high, a great reputation, he insisted that my father stayed in his ring, and that it somehow helped him, probably even saved his life.
Because he really-- under his medication or medical supervision, the supervision began to improve.
And one day, when I visited him, there was a huge crowd in front of the hospital.
And I couldn't figure out what it was, but I had to worm my way through the mob.
And when I reached the elevator guy who always-- the lift boy as they called it, the one who always took me to the fifth floor where my father was located.
I asked why there was this crowd.
And he said, you'll see in a minute.
And after about five minutes waiting in the elevator, and I got quite impatient, of course.
I heard people screaming, Heil, Heil, Heil.
And I thought first Hitler would come, but it was actually Goring.
And Goring entered the elevator with an SS man and also went to the fifth floor.
Now it just happened to be that this was a very old, creaky elevator and it got stuck between the second and third floor.
And of course, the elevator operator got very, very impatient and nervous and because the elevator simply wouldn't budge.
And then finally, the SS man got very rough with the driver and elevator operator and they had a little argument.
But then Goring said, don't worry, don't worry, everything will be OK.
It'll just take a few minutes.
And then he became aware of me and we had a little conversation.
It only lasted about three or four minutes.
And he asked me who I was visiting.
I told him my father.
And-- and he was visiting a cousin or a niece on the same floor.
And I remember when the elevator started to move again and the doors opened, there were the nurses and the doctors outside the elevator giving the Hitler salute.
And Hitler-- Goring pushed me right in front of him.
And of course, it was a very strange experience, as you can imagine, being greeted with, by about 20 people giving the Hitler salute.
No, I did not.
Neither did Goring.
He was not in a uniform, he was in a very light, white raincoat.
But that was my experience with Goring.
And then after he had visited his cousin, I had-- the door to my father's room was always open.
After he had visited his cousin, he came back through the hallway and he looked, probably-- he looked into the room where my father was together with me.
And he saw me or recognized me and he came in and shock my father's hand.
Now my father for some reason had the-- won the Iron Cross in the First World War and had the Iron Cross for some reason, I cannot remember why, on his night table.
And Goring saw it and picked it up, and it was the Iron Cross First Class.
And he patted my father on the shoulder, not realizing that he was Jewish, and wished him all the best.
And my father was absolutely in a state of shock.
Oh, he did recognize him, of course.
You cannot help but with all the SS bodyguards behind them.
So but that was my experience with Goring.
I have no idea.
You have to realize at that time in 1937, I was about 14 years old.
And I really did not ask these questions.
They did manage still to live in their huge apartment in the Kurfurstendamm, which is the Fifth Avenue of Berlin.
And it was-- I do know, for instance, that they also has started to renting rooms.
And one of the people who they rented to was a woman by the name of Marika Roekk-- R-O-E-K-K, who happened to be a movie star.
And she-- whenever I visited my father during the-- on weekends, she always had a boyfriend staying with her.
That I remember, it impressed me enormously at that age.
And he also had one of the most famous horse-back riders staying with him, too, as what they call untermieter, a lodger.
And so they make-- made ends meet.
But they could still pay child support to my mother, who also let rooms.
I'm quite sure there were some.
Now if any Jewish organizations ever helped my mother, I simply never asked.
It just didn't occur to me as a youngster to say, does any Jewish organization support us.
Maybe they did.
But I do know that my father still paid child support to us and my mother still made a little bit of money from renting rooms.
So I did not go into, at that age, into any further financial details.
Offhand, I have to be absolutely honest, offhand I can not remember a single incident where I saw any member of the SS or SA hitting or striking Jews.

I do remember during the Kristallnacht, November 11-- or November 9, 1938, that a number of SS-men painted swastikas and the star of David on Jewish stores, especially on the street, the Kaiser Allee, where I lived.
I was-- well, I was out, not during the night.
The following morning I was out and I saw the smashed windows, because that happened mostly during the night.
That's why they call it Kristall Night, Kristallnacht.
The smashing of the windows really took place after sundown.
I stayed at home, yes.
Well, I had no, except for my father, who already had had the stroke and who was home at the Kurfurstendamm.
I had no relatives in Berlin except my immediate family-- my sister and my mother.
And nothing had happened to them.
But of course, the damage was enormous as far as the stores were concerned, because it was actually all the window panes were broken.
Yes, my father had two brothers, Otto, who emigrated with his family to China, to Canton in China, where he died later of cancer.
And Ernst, who, in 1937 or 1936, I cannot remember the exact year, committed suicide because his practice was taken from him.
He was a medical doctor, too.
And on my father's side, almost the same thing happened.

My mother had two brothers, Hans Fraenkel, who moved from Italy to Switzerland, and the other uncle, or rather my mother's brother, Max, who was a medical doctor and also committed suicide because he could no longer support his family, which consisted of a wife, Lotte, and two daughters, Ilse and Ursula Fraenkel.

No, the reason why he first moved to Italy is because he became the commercial correspondent for the a newspaper, a daily newspaper in Germany, which still exists, incidentally, a very highly respected newspaper.
He became the commercial correspondent, first in Milano, in Italy.
And then later he moved because of the anti-- the fascist anti-semitic laws in 1938.
When Mussolini became a great close ally of Hitler, he moved to Switzerland to save his own life.
I do not know whether he-- they knew that he was a Jew.
I only knew he still worked until 1938 for the newspaper.
What happened after that when he moved to Switzerland, I do not know.
Well, they tried to save themselves already long before.
They tried, I think they started in 1936 or 1937.
They tried to emigrate either to the United States or to England.
But the-- the immigration visas, as far as the United States was concerned, was totally oversubscribed by about I think eight or 10 years by the time that my mother tried to emigrate.
And I remember us lining up outside the US Embassy in Berlin.
And it took us probably five or six hours to finally get to the officer in charge of emigration, or immigration.
And he said, if we could put down our names but we would have to wait about eight years until it was our number to emigrate.
